documents.title: New Mexico State University public AI hub or guidance
New Mexico State University AI policy/guidance source

ai_strategy.summary: New Mexico State University's public AI posture is visible through governance guidance, teaching resources, research activity, and enterprise/tool support. The evidence points to a pragmatic R1 approach: encourage AI literacy and research while constraining sensitive data and academic-integrity risks.

key_provisions: Use AI in ways consistent with academic integrity and existing university policy.
Protect sensitive, confidential, student, research, or regulated data when using AI tools.
Faculty or academic units retain responsibility for clear course-level expectations where applicable.
Human review and accountability remain necessary for AI-assisted work.
ai_policy.summary: New Mexico State University has public AI guidance or policy-adjacent materials. The cited guidance addresses responsible use, data protection, ethics, and course or work-context expectations.

enterprise_platforms.product: Aggie Innovation Space and research cyberinfrastructure

computing_infrastructure.summary: NMSU's Discovery HPC cluster is free for current students, faculty, and staff and has 54 compute nodes, including 16 GPU nodes. The public component table lists physical NVIDIA K40, P100, V100, A100, and T4 GPUs; counting physical GPUs gives 32 accelerators, while some A100 nodes expose MIG slices for scheduling.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: New Mexico State University responsible AI and access signal

regulatory_context.summary: New Mexico public AI activity is relevant context; this profile treats campus policy and institutional evidence as the primary governance layer.

narrative_summary: New Mexico State University presents an AI posture typical of an active R1 institution: public guidance for generative AI, visible teaching resources, research activity, and a growing set of supported tools or infrastructure. The most mature signals are governance and responsible-use guidance, teaching and syllabus support, and research capacity through DREAM Research Center and AI education collaborative. Bachelor of Science in Artificial Intelligence gives the institution a workforce-development or curriculum signal, while Aggie Innovation Space and research cyberinfrastructure shows that AI is also being handled as an enterprise technology and data-governance issue. Public sources also show attention to responsible use, access, bias, privacy, or community impact, though some operational details remain incomplete. In particular, current public pages do not consistently disclose named executive ownership, all procurement decisions, full user counts, or complete dollar amounts. This profile therefore treats the institution as operational and growing rather than fully transformative: AI is embedded across policy, pedagogy, research, and tools, but not every part of the campus AI operating model is publicly documented.

gaps_acknowledged: Leadership names, enrollment, and exact enterprise AI user counts were not filled unless directly necessary; public pages were stronger for initiatives than current-role rosters.
Some initiatives cite policy or hub pages because a single dated strategy document was not publicly identified.
No comprehensive AI budget was identified beyond explicitly cited grants or public funding signals.
NMSU discloses Discovery GPU hardware, but not enterprise AI platform usage counts, Microsoft/Google AI license costs, or student subgroup outcomes.
